passages.title: The War of 1812 in British North America
passages.body:
Between 1812 and 1815, Britain and the United States fought a war that included major battles in what is now Canada. Many people living in Upper Canada (today’s Ontario) had Loyalist roots or had arrived from the United States; they feared American invasion and wanted to remain under British rule.

British regular troops, local militia, and Indigenous allies all played important roles. Indigenous nations such as the Shawnee leader Tecumseh hoped a British alliance would help protect their lands from American expansion. At Queenston Heights, at Chrysler’s Farm, and at other sites, defenders turned back American attacks.

The war did not end with a clear winner on the battlefield, but the peace treaty signed in Europe restored borders roughly to what they had been before the fighting. For many Canadians today, the war is remembered as a time when diverse groups worked together to resist invasion and helped shape a distinct identity north of the border.

passages.title: Confederation and the Dominion of Canada
passages.body:
By the 1860s, the British colonies in North America faced political and economic pressures. Some leaders feared American expansion after the Civil War; others wanted stronger trade links and a railway to link the colonies.

At conferences in Charlottetown and Quebec City, politicians debated a federal system — powers shared between a central government and provincial governments. In 1867, the British North America Act created the Dominion of Canada with four provinces: Ontario, Quebec, Nova Scotia, and New Brunswick.

The new country could make laws about trade, defence, and banking, while provinces kept responsibility for areas such as education. Other regions joined later through negotiations. Confederation was not perfect; many voices, including those of First Nations, Métis, and Inuit peoples, were not fully represented in those early decisions, an issue Canadians still study and discuss today.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, about when did the War of 1812 take place?
Why did many people in Upper Canada fear American invasion?
choices: They wanted to join the United States immediately
They wished to remain under British rule and worried about losing that
questions.answer: They wished to remain under British rule and worried about losing that

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what did Tecumseh and some Indigenous allies hope to achieve by working with the British?
choices: To move all settlers to Europe
To help protect their lands from American expansion
questions.answer: To help protect their lands from American expansion

questions.prompt: Which of the following groups does the passage say helped defend British North America?
choices: Only the British navy in Europe
British troops, militia, and Indigenous allies
questions.answer: British troops, militia, and Indigenous allies

questions.prompt: The passage says the peace treaty —
choices: moved Canada into the United States
restored borders roughly to what they had been before the war
ended British rule in North America
questions.answer: restored borders roughly to what they had been before the war

questions.prompt: What is one way the passage says the war is remembered in Canada today?
choices: As a time when no one cooperated
As a time when diverse groups resisted invasion and helped shape identity
As a war Canada lost completely
questions.answer: As a time when diverse groups resisted invasion and helped shape identity

questions.prompt: The word "invasion" in the passage is closest in meaning to —
choices: an armed attack into another’s territory
questions.answer: an armed attack into another’s territory

questions.prompt: Which title best matches the main idea of the first passage?
choices: The War of 1812 and Its Meaning for Early British North America
Why the United States Won Every War
questions.answer: The War of 1812 and Its Meaning for Early British North America

questions.prompt: According to the passage, one reason leaders discussed uniting the colonies was —
choices: concern about American expansion after the U.S. Civil War
to remove education from every province
questions.answer: concern about American expansion after the U.S. Civil War

questions.prompt: In 1867, how many provinces formed the original Dominion of Canada, according to the passage?
Which document does the passage say created the Dominion of Canada?
According to the passage, which area would provinces likely control under Confederation?
choices: Only banking for the whole country

questions.prompt: What does the passage suggest about early Confederation decisions?
choices: Some groups, including many Indigenous peoples, were not fully represented
questions.answer: Some groups, including many Indigenous peoples, were not fully represented

questions.prompt: The word "federal" in the passage refers to a system where —
choices: power is shared between central and provincial governments
questions.answer: power is shared between central and provincial governments
questions.hintContext: The passage contrasts a central government with provincial governments.

questions.prompt: Which statement would the author most likely agree with?
choices: Understanding Confederation includes studying who was left out of early decisions
questions.answer: Understanding Confederation includes studying who was left out of early decisions

questions.prompt: Statement: "If a plant is not watered for a month, it will die." This plant was not watered for a month and is still alive. What can you conclude?
choices: The statement must be false in this case
questions.answer: The statement must be false in this case
questions.hintContext: A general statement is tested by a specific counterexample.

questions.prompt: Statement: "Every student who wears a red hat is a member of the choir." Maria wears a red hat. Can you conclude she is in the choir?
choices: Not necessarily — the statement does not say only choir members wear red hats
Yes, because red is the best colour
questions.answer: Not necessarily — the statement does not say only choir members wear red hats

questions.prompt: Which is the strongest evidence for the claim "Our school should start a composting program"?
choices: Food waste makes up a large share of landfill waste and composting reduces it
questions.answer: Food waste makes up a large share of landfill waste and composting reduces it

questions.prompt: If all bloops are razzles, and this is not a razzle, then —
questions.hintContext: Bloops are a subset of razzles. If something is not a razzle, can it be a bloop?

questions.prompt: A friend says: "I flipped a coin three times and got heads each time, so the next flip must be tails." What is wrong with this reasoning?
choices: Each fair flip is independent — past results do not force the next outcome
questions.answer: Each fair flip is independent — past results do not force the next outcome
